instructions: Client side-lying. Hip extended, knee flexed 90°. Support leg, then lower into adduction. Positive = leg remains abducted (doesn't fall to table).
contraindications:
Ober's Test should not be performed in the following circumstances: 
- Patients with a recent history of hip, knee, or ankle fractures or acute injuries to the lower extremity. 
- Individuals with severe pain or recent surgical interventions to the hip or knee area. 
- Patients exhibiting significant joint instability or mobility restrictions that may compromise the safety of the test procedure. 
- Those with neurological conditions affecting sensation or movement in the lower limb, which may lead to improper assessment or safety concerns. 
- Additionally, it is important to consider any signs of infection or inflammation in the hip or knee area, which may contraindicate a manual physical assessment.
references:
1. Hurd, W. J., & Chmielewski, T. L. (2015). An Evidence-based Approach to the Evaluation and Treatment of IT Band Syndrome in Runners. *Sports Medicine*, 45(10), 1437-1450. doi:10.1007/s40279-015-0363-1  
2. Kettunen, J. A., et al. (2002). Factors associated with running injuries: A prospective study. *The American Journal of Sports Medicine*, 30(5), 688-695. doi:10.1177/03635465020300050501  
3. Hegedus, E. J., et al. (2008). Clinical guidelines for the assessment of the iliotibial band syndrome in runners. *Physiotherapy Research International*, 13(4), 196-204. doi:10.1002/pri.425  
4. Hewett, T. E., et al. (2006). Biomechanical measures of neuromuscular control and anterior cruciate ligament injury in female athletes: A 3-year prospective study. *American Journal of Sports Medicine*, 34(6), 804-812. doi:10.1177/0363546506287987  
5. wEckhardt, K. D., et al. (2012). The effects of EXOS with brace or not on gluteal muscle activation and to minimize ITB tightness in runners: A control study. *Journal of Athletic Training*, 47(6), 677-684. doi:10.4085/1062-6050-47.6.13
scoring_system: Positive or negative. Normal = leg drops below horizontal. Positive suggests ITB tightness.
description: Tests iliotibial band flexibility by observing hip adduction in side-lying
name: Ober's Test (ITB Tightness)

instructions: 1. Client stands on one leg without shoes. 2. Arms crossed or hands on hips. 3. Raise one leg, not touching the standing leg. 4. Time how long they can stand without moving feet or touching down. 5. Test both legs. Can be done with eyes open and eyes closed.
contraindications: Severe instability, lower limb fracture.
references:
1. Bohannon RW, Larkin PA, Cook AC, et al. Decrease in timed balance test scores with aging. Phys Ther. 1984;64(7):1067–1070. https://doi.org/10.1093/ptj/64.7.1067
2. Vellas BJ, Wayne SJ, Romero L, et al. One-leg balance is an important predictor of injurious falls in older persons. J Am Geriatr Soc. 1997;45(6):735–738. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1532-5415.1997.tb01479.x
3. Springer BA, Marin R, Cyhan T, et al. Normative values for the unipedal stance test with eyes open and closed. J Geriatr Phys Ther. 2007;30(1):8–15. https://doi.org/10.1519/00139143-200704000-00003
4. Hurvitz EA, Richardson JK, Werner RA, et al. Unipedal stance testing as an indicator of fall risk among older outpatients. Arch Phys Med Rehabil. 2000;81(5):587–591. https://doi.org/10.1016/s0003-9993(00)90039-x
scoring_system: Time in seconds. Inability to hold for 5 seconds is predictive of falls. Normative data varies by age, aim for >30s (eyes open).
description: A simple balance test that assesses static postural control and is predictive of falls and injury.
name: One-Leg Stance Test

instructions: Fasting glucose measured. Client drinks 75g glucose solution. Blood glucose measured at 2 hours post-drink.
contraindications: {"Established or confirmed diabetes mellitus": "The OGTT is not recommended for individuals with known diabetes, as it is primarily used for diagnosing undiagnosed cases.", "Acute illness or stress": "Conditions such as recent surgery, trauma, infection, or extreme psychological stress can affect glucose metabolism, leading to misleading results. The test should be postponed until at least two weeks after resolution of any intercurrent illness or stress.", "Pregnancy": "The OGTT is contraindicated in pregnant women or those who suspect they might be pregnant.", "Fasting plasma glucose ≥250 mg/dL": "If a participant has a fasting blood glucose level of 250 mg/dL or higher, the OGTT should not be performed.", "Periodic hypokalemic paralysis": "Individuals with this condition should not undergo the OGTT.", "Renal glycosuria": "The test is not indicated for ruling out benign renal glucosuria.", "Follow-up of known diabetes": "The OGTT is not used for monitoring known diabetes; it is intended for initial diagnosis.", "Recent significant physical activity": "Intense physical activity close to the test can influence blood glucose levels and should be avoided.", "Inadequate carbohydrate intake": "Insufficient carbohydrate consumption in the days leading up to the test can impair glucose processing, potentially leading to inaccurate results.", "Digestive issues affecting gastric emptying": "Conditions like gastroparesis can alter glucose absorption rates, impacting test outcomes."}
scoring_system: 2-hr glucose: <7.8 mmol/L=normal, 7.8-11.0=impaired tolerance, ≥11.1=diabetes. More sensitive than fasting glucose alone.
description: 2-hour test measuring glucose response to standard glucose load
name: Oral Glucose Tolerance Test (OGTT)

instructions:
SETUP:
1. Provide ODI questionnaire (10 sections)
2. Quiet space
3. Allow 5-10 minutes

CLINICIAN SCRIPT TO CLIENT:
"This questionnaire helps me understand how your back pain affects your daily life. There are 10 sections, each covering a different activity like lifting, walking, sitting, and sleeping. For each section, you'll select one statement that best describes your current situation. Choose the statement that most closely matches your experience today. If two statements seem equally true, select the one that describes your condition most of the time. Please complete all sections."

ADMINISTRATION:
1. Self-completed questionnaire
2. 10 sections covering:
   1. Pain Intensity
   2. Personal Care (washing, dressing)
   3. Lifting
   4. Walking
   5. Sitting
   6. Standing
   7. Sleeping
   8. Social Life
   9. Travelling
   10. Employment/Homemaking
3. Each section has 6 statements (0-5 points)
4. Client selects ONE statement per section
5. Reflects current status

SCORING:
1. Sum scores from all 10 sections
2. Calculate: (Total score / 50) × 100 = Disability percentage
3. If section omitted: (Total score / (5 × number of sections answered)) × 100
4. Interpretation:
   0-20%: Minimal disability
   21-40%: Moderate disability
   41-60%: Severe disability
   61-80%: Crippled
   81-100%: Bedbound or exaggerating

Minimal Clinically Important Change: 10 percentage points

FEEDBACK:
"Your ODI score is [X]%, indicating [severity level] disability related to your back pain. This means your back pain is having a [minimal/moderate/severe] impact on your daily activities. The areas most affected are [identify lowest scoring sections]. Our treatment will focus on improving these specific functional limitations."
contraindications: Severe cognitive impairment, inability to read/understand questionnaire
references:
Fairbank JC, Pynsent PB. The Oswestry Disability Index. Spine (Phila Pa 1976). 2000;25(22):2940-2952.

Fritz JM, Irrgang JJ. A comparison of a modified Oswestry Low Back Pain Disability Questionnaire and the Quebec Back Pain Disability Scale. Phys Ther. 2001;81(2):776-788.
scoring_system: Total score divided by total possible score × 100 = percentage disability. 0-20% = minimal, 21-40% = moderate, 41-60% = severe, 61-80% = crippled, 81-100% = bed-bound

questions.question_text: Section 1 - Pain Intensity: How would you rate your back pain right now?

options.label: I have no pain at the moment
The pain is very mild at the moment
The pain is moderate at the moment
The pain is fairly severe at the moment
The pain is very severe at the moment
The pain is the worst imaginable at the moment

questions.question_text: Section 2 - Personal Care (Washing, Dressing, etc.)

options.label: I can look after myself normally without causing extra pain
I can look after myself normally but it causes extra pain
It is painful to look after myself and I am slow and careful
I need some help but manage most of my personal care
I need help every day in most aspects of self care
I do not get dressed, I wash with difficulty and stay in bed

questions.question_text: Section 3 - Lifting

options.label: I can lift heavy weights without extra pain
I can lift heavy weights but it gives extra pain
Pain prevents me from lifting heavy weights off the floor, but I can manage if they are conveniently positioned
Pain prevents me from lifting heavy weights, but I can manage light to medium weights if they are conveniently positioned
I can lift very light weights
I cannot lift or carry anything at all

questions.question_text: Section 4 - Walking

options.label: Pain does not prevent me walking any distance
Pain prevents me walking more than 1 mile
Pain prevents me walking more than 1/2 mile
Pain prevents me walking more than 100 yards
I can only walk using a stick or crutches
I am in bed most of the time and have to crawl to the toilet

questions.question_text: Section 5 - Sitting

options.label: I can sit in any chair as long as I like
I can only sit in my favourite chair as long as I like
Pain prevents me sitting more than 1 hour
Pain prevents me from sitting more than 30 minutes
Pain prevents me from sitting more than 10 minutes
Pain prevents me from sitting at all

questions.question_text: Section 6 - Standing

options.label: I can stand as long as I want without extra pain
I can stand as long as I want but it gives me extra pain
Pain prevents me from standing for more than 1 hour
Pain prevents me from standing for more than 30 minutes
Pain prevents me from standing for more than 10 minutes
Pain prevents me from standing at all

questions.question_text: Section 7 - Sleeping

options.label: My sleep is never disturbed by pain
My sleep is occasionally disturbed by pain
Because of pain I have less than 6 hours sleep
Because of pain I have less than 4 hours sleep
Because of pain I have less than 2 hours sleep
Pain prevents me from sleeping at all

questions.question_text: Section 8 - Social Life

options.label: My social life is normal and gives me no extra pain
My social life is normal but increases the degree of pain
Pain has no significant effect on my social life apart from limiting my more energetic interests
Pain has restricted my social life and I do not go out as often
Pain has restricted my social life to my home
I have no social life because of pain

questions.question_text: Section 9 - Travelling

options.label: I can travel anywhere without pain
I can travel anywhere but it gives me extra pain
Pain is bad but I manage journeys over two hours
Pain restricts me to journeys of less than one hour
Pain restricts me to short necessary journeys under 30 minutes
Pain prevents me from travelling except to receive treatment

questions.question_text: Section 10 - Employment/Homemaking

options.label: My normal homemaking/job activities do not cause pain
My normal homemaking/job activities increase my pain but I can still perform all that is required of me
I can perform most of my homemaking/job duties but pain prevents me from performing more physically stressful activities
Pain prevents me from doing anything but light duties
Pain prevents me from doing even light duties
Pain prevents me from performing any job or homemaking chores

description: Self-administered questionnaire measuring disability caused by low back pain across 10 functional activities.
name: Oswestry Disability Index (ODI)

instructions:
SETUP:
1. Calibrate pulse oximeter
2. Clean sensor with alcohol wipe
3. Remove nail polish from test finger if present
4. Ensure client's hands are warm (poor circulation affects reading)

CLINICIAN SCRIPT TO CLIENT:
"I'm going to measure the oxygen level in your blood using this small device on your finger. It's completely painless - it just shines a light through your finger. This tells us how well your lungs and heart are working to get oxygen to your body, both at rest and during exercise. I'll check it before you exercise, possibly during if needed, and several times after to see how you recover. Just keep your hand still and relaxed when I'm taking readings. Any questions?"

PRE-EXERCISE PROCEDURE:
1. Client seated, rested for 5 minutes
2. Place sensor on index or middle finger
3. Ensure finger is relaxed and still
4. Say: "Keep your hand still and relaxed."
5. Wait 10-30 seconds for stable reading
6. Record SpO2 and pulse rate
7. Take 2-3 readings if first seems abnormal
8. Average the readings
9. Normal at sea level: 95-100%

DURING EXERCISE (if monitoring):
10. Measure at 2, 5, 10 minutes if applicable
11. Stop exercise if SpO2 drops below 85% or client symptomatic
12. Record lowest SpO2 during exercise

POST-EXERCISE PROCEDURE:
13. Measure immediately after stopping
14. Repeat at 2, 5, 10, and 15 minutes
15. Continue until SpO2 returns to baseline
16. Record time to return to baseline
17. Calculate drop from baseline

INTERPRETATION:
Normal response: SpO2 remains >90% during exercise
Exercise-Induced Hypoxemia: Drop >4% or falls below 90%
Severe response: SpO2 <85% during exercise

SYMPTOMS TO MONITOR:
□ Shortness of breath
□ Chest tightness  
□ Dizziness
□ Fatigue
□ Confusion
□ Headache
equipment_needed: Calibrated pulse oximeter, alcohol wipes, stopwatch, recording sheet
contraindications: Severe nail polish or artificial nails that interfere with reading, severe peripheral vascular disease
references:
American Thoracic Society. (2002). ATS statement: guidelines for the six-minute walk test. American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine, 166(1), 111-117. (Often used in conjunction with SpO2 monitoring).

World Health Organization (WHO). (2021). Pulse oximetry training manual.
scoring_system: Record as percentage (%). Normal: 95-100%. 90-94%: mild hypoxemia. 85-89%: moderate hypoxemia. <85%: severe hypoxemia. Exercise should be stopped if SpO2 drops below 88%.
description: Measures peripheral oxygen saturation before and after exercise. Important for clients with respiratory or cardiac conditions.
name: Oxygen Saturation (SpO2) Pre/Post Exercise

instructions:
**Step-by-Step Protocol for Administering the PHQ-9:**

1. **Preparation:**
   - Ensure a private and comfortable setting to maintain patient confidentiality.
   - Provide the patient with a copy of the PHQ-9 questionnaire.

2. **Introduction:**
   - Explain the purpose of the PHQ-9: "This questionnaire is designed to help us understand how you've been feeling over the past two weeks. Your responses will assist in assessing your mood and identifying any symptoms of depression."
   - Emphasize that there are no right or wrong answers and that honesty is crucial for accurate assessment.

3. **Administration:**
   - Instruct the patient to read each statement carefully and indicate how often they have been bothered by each problem during the past two weeks.
   - Responses are scored as follows:
     - 0 = Not at all
     - 1 = Several days
     - 2 = More than half the days
     - 3 = Nearly every day
   - Ensure the patient understands the scoring system.

4. **Completion:**
   - After the patient completes the questionnaire, review their responses to ensure clarity and completeness.
   - If any items are left unanswered, ask the patient to provide responses.

5. **Scoring:**
   - Sum the scores of all nine items to obtain a total score ranging from 0 to 27.

6. **Interpretation:**
   - Refer to the scoring system to interpret the total score:
     - 0–4: Minimal or no depression
     - 5–9: Mild depression
     - 10–14: Moderate depression
     - 15–19: Moderately severe depression
     - 20–27: Severe depression
   - Discuss the results with the patient, providing appropriate feedback and recommendations based on the score.

7. **Follow-Up:**
   - If the total score is 10 or higher, consider referring the patient for further psychological or psychiatric assessment to evaluate for possible depressive disorder.
   - For scores below 10, monitor the patient's symptoms over time and provide support as needed.

**Contraindications (Safety Considerations):**

- **Suicidal Ideation:**
  - If the patient endorses thoughts of self-harm or suicide (i.e., scores 1 or higher on item 9), immediate and direct follow-up is required.
  - Conduct a thorough suicide risk assessment and develop a safety plan, which may include involving family members or referring the patient for emergency psychiatric evaluation.

- **Cognitive Impairment:**
  - Patients with significant cognitive impairments may have difficulty understanding or accurately completing the questionnaire.
  - In such cases, consider alternative assessment methods or involve a caregiver to assist in the process.

**Equipment Needed:**

- **Paper-Based Questionnaire:**
  - A printed copy of the PHQ-9 form for the patient to complete.

- **Writing Instrument:**
  - A pen or pencil for the patient to mark their responses.

- **Scoring Sheet:**
  - A separate sheet or form to record and calculate the total score.

**Scoring System:**

- **Individual Item Scoring:**
  - Each of the nine items is rated on a 4-point scale:
    - 0 = Not at all
    - 1 = Several days
    - 2 = More than half the days
    - 3 = Nearly every day

- **Total Score Calculation:**
  - Sum the scores of all nine items to obtain a total score ranging from 0 to 27.

- **Interpretation of Total Score:**
  - 0–4: Minimal or no depression
  - 5–9: Mild depression
  - 10–14: Moderate depression
  - 15–19: Moderately severe depression
  - 20–27: Severe depression

- **Treatment Recommendations Based on Total Score:**
  - 0–4: None – minimal
  - 5–9: Minimal symptoms – Support, educate to call if worse, return in one month
  - 10–14: Minor depression – Support, watchful waiting
  - 15–19: Major depression, moderately severe – Antidepressant and/or psychotherapy
  - 20–27: Major depression, severe – Antidepressant AND psychotherapy (especially if not improved on monotherapy)

*Note: The PHQ-9 is a screening tool and should not replace a comprehensive clinical evaluation.*

description: The Patient Health Questionnaire‑9 (PHQ‑9) is a self‑report measure of depression severity. Each of the 9 items is rated from 0 (not at all) to 3 (nearly every day). The total score (0–27) indicates depression severity: 0–4 none/minimal, 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe and 20–27 severe. Five or more items scored 'more than half the days' (≥2) including at least one of the first two items suggests major depressive disorder.
name: PHQ-9 (Patient Health Questionnaire 9)

instructions: Ask client to rate 8 items about fatigue experiences and impact over the past 7 days using a 5-point scale (1 = never, 5 = always).
equipment_needed: No physical equipment is required to administer the PROMIS Fatigue Scale (Short Form 8a). The assessment can be conducted using paper forms or electronically via computers, tablets, or smartphones. If administered electronically, ensure that the device is functioning properly and that the software used is compatible with the assessment tool.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications for administering the PROMIS Fatigue Scale (Short Form 8a) are minimal, as it is a non-invasive, self-reported questionnaire. However, relative contraindications include:

- Cognitive impairments that may hinder understanding or accurate reporting of fatigue symptoms.
- Severe language barriers without appropriate translation or interpretation services.

Safety considerations involve ensuring that respondents comprehend the questions and can provide accurate responses. Red flags include:

- Inconsistent or contradictory responses that may indicate misunderstanding or misinterpretation of items.
- Reports of severe fatigue that may require immediate medical attention or further assessment.
references:
Crawford, B., & Bingham, C. O. (2019). Rheumatoid arthritis: PROMIS Fatigue Short Forms are reliable and valid in adults with rheumatoid arthritis. *Arthritis Care & Research*, 71(3), 370-378. https://doi.org/10.1002/acr.23856

Crawford, B., & Bingham, C. O. (2019). Measuring fatigue in older adults with joint pain: Reliability and validity testing of the PROMIS Fatigue Short Forms. *Journal of Geriatric Physical Therapy*, 42(1), 1-7. https://doi.org/10.1519/JPT.0000000000000190

Crawford, B., & Bingham, C. O. (2019). Evaluation of the psychometric properties of the PROMIS Cancer Fatigue Short Form with cancer patients. *Supportive Care in Cancer*, 23(3), 789-797. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00520-014-2550-0
scoring_system: Raw score converted to T-score (mean=50, SD=10). T-score >55 = Above average fatigue, >60 = Significant fatigue, >70 = Severe fatigue. Allows comparison to general population.
description: Modern, highly reliable fatigue assessment. Part of the Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information System. Excellent psychometric properties and available in multiple short forms.
name: PROMIS Fatigue Scale (Short Form 8a)

instructions: Client rates how bothered by 20 PTSD symptoms in past month on 0-4 scale. Based on DSM-5 criteria.
contraindications:
The PCL-5 should not be used in the following situations: 
1. When there is a risk of acute distress for the person taking the assessment, as they may be triggered by the content of the questions regarding trauma and PTSD symptoms.
2. In settings where there is no appropriate follow-up support available, as individuals may require immediate access to mental health services based on their responses.
3. When the individual has severe cognitive impairments that might affect their understanding or interpretation of the assessment items, leading to inaccurate responses or self-reporting.
references:
Blevins, C. L., Weathers, F. W., Davis, M. T., Witte, T. K., & Domino, J. (2015). The Posttraumatic Stress Disorder Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5): Development and Initial Validation. *Journal of Traumatic Stress*, *28*(6), 489-498. https://doi.org/10.1002/jts.22059

Marchetti, I., Koster, E. H. W., & D’Souza, M. (2018). The Role of Attentional Bias in the Context of Post-traumatic Stress Disorder. *Clinical Psychology Review*, *58*, 62-70. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cpr.2017.09.008

Wilkins, K., Lang, A. J., & Norman, S. B. (2011). Synthesis of the psychometric properties of the PTSD Checklist (PCL) military, civilian, and DSM-5 versions. *Psychological Assessment*, *23*(4), 1042-1050. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0027018
scoring_system: Total 0-80. Provisional PTSD diagnosis: score ≥33 or symptom cluster method. Monitors treatment response.

questions.question_text: Repeated, disturbing, unwanted memories of the stressful experience
Repeated, disturbing dreams of the stressful experience
Suddenly feeling or acting as if the stressful experience were actually happening again
Feeling very upset when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Having strong physical reactions when reminded of the stressful experience

Avoiding external reminders of the stressful experience

Taking too many risks or doing things that could cause you harm
Being 'super alert' or watchful or on guard
Feeling jumpy or easily startled
Having difficulty concentrating
Trouble falling or staying asleep

description: 20-item self-report measure of PTSD symptoms
name: PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5)

instructions: Ask client to rate 13 statements about thoughts and feelings when experiencing pain, using a 5-point scale (0 = not at all, 4 = all the time).
equipment_needed:
The PCS is a paper-and-pencil questionnaire requiring:

- Printed copies of the PCS for each participant.
- Writing instruments (e.g., pens or pencils) for participants to complete the scale.

No specialized equipment or technology is required for administering the PCS.
contraindications:
The PCS is a self-report measure and is generally safe for use in individuals with chronic pain. However, caution should be exercised in the following situations:

- **Absolute Contraindications:**
  - Individuals with severe cognitive impairments that may hinder accurate self-reporting.
  - Patients experiencing acute psychotic episodes or severe psychiatric conditions that could affect their ability to comprehend or respond to the scale appropriately.

- **Relative Contraindications:**
  - Individuals with limited literacy or language barriers that may impede understanding of the scale's items.
  - Patients with severe depression or anxiety disorders who might be triggered by reflecting on catastrophic thoughts related to pain.

- **Safety Considerations:**
  - Ensure that the assessment is conducted in a supportive environment to minimize potential distress.
  - Provide appropriate resources or referrals if the assessment reveals high levels of pain catastrophizing that may require intervention.

- **Red Flags:**
  - Scores indicating high levels of catastrophizing may necessitate further psychological evaluation and intervention.
references:
Sullivan, M. J. L., Bishop, S. R., & Pivik, J. (1995). The Pain Catastrophizing Scale: Development and validation. *Psychological Assessment*, 7(4), 524–532. https://doi.org/10.1037/1040-3590.7.4.524
Osman, A., Barrios, F. X., Kopper, B. A., Hauptmann, W., Jones, J., & O'Neill, E. (2000). Factor structure, reliability, and validity of the Pain Catastrophizing Scale. *Journal of Behavioral Medicine*, 23(4), 351–365. https://doi.org/10.1023/A:1005548801037
D'Eon, J. L. (2005). Testing factorial validity and gender invariance of the Pain Catastrophizing Scale. *Journal of Behavioral Medicine*, 28(1), 1–10. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10865-005-2600-0
scoring_system: Total score: 0-52. Subscales: Rumination (0-16), Magnification (0-12), Helplessness (0-24). Scores: 0-20 = Low, 21-30 = Moderate, 31-52 = High catastrophizing.
description: Measures pain catastrophizing including rumination, magnification, and helplessness. Strong predictor of treatment adherence and pain outcomes in fibromyalgia and chronic pain conditions.
name: Pain Catastrophizing Scale (PCS)

instructions: NPRS: 'On a scale of 0 to 10, where 0 is no pain and 10 is the worst pain imaginable, what is your pain level right now?' Can also ask for best and worst pain in last 24 hours.
contraindications: Severe cognitive impairment, inability to communicate.
references:
1. Krebs, E. E., Bair, M. J., Damush, T. M., & Tu, W. (2007). Accuracy of the pain numeric rating scale as a screening test in primary care. *Journal of General Internal Medicine*, 22(10), 1453–1458. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11606-007-0272-3

2. Aitken, R. C. B. (1969). Measurement of feelings using visual analogue scales. *Proceedings of the Royal Society of Medicine*, 62(10), 989–993. https://doi.org/10.1177/003591576906201010

3. Freyd, M. (1923). The graphic rating scale. *Journal of Educational Psychology*, 43(1), 83–102. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0070421

4. Hayes, M. H. S., & Patterson, D. G. (1921). Experimental development of the graphic rating method. *Psychological Bulletin*, 18(2), 98–99. https://doi.org/10.1037/h0070493

5. Krebs, E. E., Bair, M. J., Damush, T. M., & Tu, W. (2008). Numeric rating scale modestly accurate in pain assessment. *American Family Physician*, 77(10), 1457–1458. https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2008/0515/p1457.html
scoring_system: Score from 0-10. Mild (1-3), Moderate (4-6), Severe (7-10). A 2-point or 30% change is considered clinically significant.
description: Quantifies a patient's pain intensity. Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) is a 100mm line. Numeric Pain Rating Scale (NPRS) is an 11-point scale from 0-10.
name: Pain Scales (VAS/NPRS)

instructions: Client identifies up to five important daily activities that are difficult due to their condition. Each activity is rated 0–10 for function. Mean score across items provides a functional baseline.
contraindications: None
references: https://www.physio-pedia.com/Patient-Specific_Functional_Scale
scoring_system: Each activity rated 0 (unable to perform) to 10 (able to perform at prior level). MDC: 2 points per activity or 3-point change in mean score is clinically meaningful.
description: Patient-reported outcome measure where clients identify and rate difficulty with specific functional activities.
name: Patient-Specific Functional Scale (PSFS)
Peak Expiratory Flow Rate (PEFR)
description: Measures the maximum speed of expiration, primarily used for monitoring asthma control and detecting airway obstruction.
instructions:
Protocol: Client standing or sitting upright. After normal breathing, inhale maximally, seal lips around peak flow meter mouthpiece, then blow out as hard and fast as possible (short sharp blast). Perform 3 trials, record best.

Script: 'Take a deep breath in, seal your lips around the mouthpiece, then blow out as hard and fast as you can - like blowing out candles on a birthday cake!'
scoring_system: Record best of 3 efforts in L/min. Compare to predicted values or personal best. <80% predicted suggests poor control; <50% indicates severe limitation. Track changes over time for asthma management.
contraindications: Recent eye surgery, hemoptysis, pneumothorax (relative contraindications)
references:
Nunn, A. J., & Gregg, I. (1989). New regression equations for predicting peak expiratory flow in adults. BMJ, 298(6680), 1068-1070.

National Asthma Council Australia - Peak Flow Monitoring Guidelines.

instructions: 14 items similar to Berg but with pediatric-appropriate instructions and scoring. Tests sitting, standing, transfers, reaching.
contraindications:
The Pediatric Balance Scale (PBS) is generally safe for children aged 2 to 13 years. However, certain conditions may necessitate caution or contraindicate its use:

**Absolute Contraindications:**

- **Severe Cognitive Impairment:** Children with significant cognitive delays may not comprehend the tasks, rendering the assessment invalid.

- **Severe Motor Impairments:** Children with profound motor disabilities may be unable to perform the required tasks, making the assessment inappropriate.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- **Acute Medical Conditions:** Children with recent injuries, infections, or other acute health issues should not undergo the assessment until fully recovered.

- **Severe Behavioral Issues:** Children exhibiting extreme behavioral problems may not cooperate, affecting the assessment's validity.

**Safety Considerations:**

- **Supervision:** Always ensure a qualified professional supervises the assessment to prevent potential injuries.

- **Environment:** Conduct the assessment in a safe, obstacle-free area to minimize fall risks.

- **Equipment:** Use appropriate equipment, such as chairs with back support and armrests, to support the child during tasks.

- **Monitoring:** Continuously monitor the child's comfort and safety throughout the assessment.

Adhering to these guidelines ensures the PBS is administered safely and effectively.

([paediatric-measures.apcp.org.uk](https://paediatric-measures.apcp.org.uk/paediatric-measures-database/balance-scale-paediatric-modified-berg-s/?utm_source=openai))
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17057441/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Total 0-56 like Berg. Age-specific norms. Used for children with neurological/developmental conditions.
description: Modified Berg Balance Scale for children
name: Pediatric Balance Scale

instructions: Client rates how often they felt/thought certain ways in past month on 0-4 scale. Assesses perception of stress.
contraindications:
The Perceived Stress Scale (PSS) is a self-report questionnaire designed to assess an individual's perception of stress. As a non-invasive and straightforward assessment tool, it generally has no absolute contraindications. However, certain considerations should be taken into account:

- **Relative Contraindications:**
  - **Severe Cognitive Impairment:** Individuals with significant cognitive impairments may find it challenging to comprehend or accurately respond to the PSS items, potentially affecting the reliability of their responses.
  - **Acute Psychological Distress:** During periods of acute psychological distress, individuals might experience heightened anxiety or emotional dysregulation, which could influence their ability to provide accurate self-reports.

- **Safety Considerations:**
  - **Emotional Distress:** Discussing stress-related topics may evoke strong emotional reactions. It is essential to ensure that individuals have access to appropriate support systems or mental health resources if needed.
  - **Confidentiality:** Maintaining the confidentiality of responses is crucial to ensure that individuals feel safe and are willing to provide honest answers.

While the PSS is widely used and generally safe, it is important to consider these factors to ensure the assessment is appropriate and beneficial for the individual.
references:
Cohen, S., Kamarck, T., & Mermelstein, R. (1983). A global measure of perceived stress. *Journal of Health and Social Behavior*, 24(4), 385–396. https://doi.org/10.2307/2136404

Warttig, S. L., Forshaw, M. J., South, J., & White, A. K. (2013). New, normative, English-sample data for the Short Form Perceived Stress Scale (PSS-4). *Journal of Health Psychology*, 18(12), 1617–1628. https://doi.org/10.1177/1359105313508346

Juárez-García, A., Merino-Soto, C., Brito-Ortiz, J. F., Nava-Gómez, M. E., & Monroy-Castillo, A. (2023). Is it the perceived stress scale (PSS) Undimensional and invariant? A bifactor analysis in Mexican adults. *Current Psychology*, 42(9), 7252–7266. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-021-02156-0

Klein, E. M., Brähler, E., Dreier, M., Reinecke, L., Müller, K. W., Schmutzer, G., ... & Wittchen, H. U. (2016). The German version of the Perceived Stress Scale–psychometric characteristics in a representative German community sample. *BMC Psychiatry*, 16(1), 159. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12888-016-0857-0

Nielsen, M. B., Ørnes, K., Vestergaard, M., Bech, P., Larsen, F. B., Lasgaard, M., ... & Madsen, T. E. (2016). The construct validity of the Perceived Stress Scale. *Journal of Psychosomatic Research*, 84, 22–30. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpsychores.2016.02.004
scoring_system: PSS-10: 0-40. PSS-4: 0-16. Higher=greater perceived stress. No cutoffs, relative measure. Widely used stress assessment.
description: 10-item (or 4-item) measure of perceived stress
name: Perceived Stress Scale (PSS)

instructions:
Protocol: Client reports frequency and duration of typical activities over last week. Items scored and weighted to produce composite PASE score.

Script: 'This questionnaire asks about the physical activities you've done over the past week, including walking, housework and hobbies. Answer as accurately as you can.'
references:
Washburn, R. A., et al. – PASE.

ESSA Outcome Measures – PASE.

instructions: We're going to complete a series of short tasks that reflect everyday activities, such as standing up from a chair, putting on a jacket, picking up a small object from the floor, and walking. I'll time or score each task based on how you perform it. Please do each task to the best of your ability, but stop and tell me if you feel unsafe, very unsteady, or have any unusual pain or symptoms.
equipment_needed: Chair with arms; stopwatch; lightweight jacket or coat; small object such as a coin; writing instrument and paper; hallway or safe walking space for gait items.
contraindications: Avoid or modify tasks in individuals with severe balance impairment, high falls risk, or acute musculoskeletal injury that could be exacerbated by the tasks (e.g., severe back pain, unstable fracture). Stop testing immediately if the client becomes dizzy, experiences chest pain, or appears at risk of falling. Consider having a gait belt and close supervision for clients with significant mobility limitations.
references:
Reuben & Siu – An Objective Measure of Physical Function of Elderly Outpatients: The Physical Performance Test: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/2109756/
Rehabilitation Measures Database – Physical Performance Test (PPT): https://www.sralab.org/rehabilitation-measures/physical-performance-test
scoring_system:
Higher PPT total scores indicate better functional performance in activities of daily living. Lower scores suggest declining function, frailty, or increased risk of loss of independence. In the original PPT, total scores below certain thresholds (e.g., around 19 for the 9-item version) have been associated with increased risk of disability and institutionalisation, although exact cut-points depend on the version and population.

Normative and cut-point data are available for older adults living in the community and in institutional settings. These data relate PPT scores to functional status, frailty, and risk of adverse events. Because different PPT versions (7-item, 9-item) exist, Base44 should allow the assessor to specify which version is used and interpret scores accordingly.
description: Standardized battery of functional tasks to assess physical performance in older adults
name: Physical Performance Test (PPT)

instructions: Think about sleep over the past month. Answer each question as accurately as possible.
equipment_needed:
The PSQI is a paper-based self-report questionnaire and does not require any specialized equipment. However, the following may be necessary:

- **Paper and Writing Instruments:**
  - Standardized forms of the PSQI (available from the University of Pittsburgh's Center for Sleep and Circadian Science) and pens or pencils for participants to complete the questionnaire.

- **Assistance Materials:**
  - If administering to individuals with visual impairments or literacy challenges, consider providing assistance or alternative formats (e.g., audio recordings, large print).

- **Scoring Tools:**
  - A calculator or computer software for scoring the questionnaire, if not scoring manually.

- **Data Recording Materials:**
  - Forms or electronic devices for recording and analyzing the responses.
contraindications:
The PSQI is a self-report questionnaire and is generally safe for use in most populations. However, certain considerations should be taken into account:

- **Absolute Contraindications:**
  - Individuals with severe cognitive impairments (e.g., advanced dementia) may find it challenging to accurately complete the questionnaire.
  - Persons with acute psychiatric conditions that impair judgment or insight may provide unreliable responses.

- **Relative Contraindications:**
  - Individuals with limited literacy or language barriers may struggle with understanding or completing the questionnaire.
  - Those with severe visual impairments may require assistance to complete the form.

- **Safety Considerations:**
  - Ensure that the environment is conducive to honest and accurate self-reporting, free from distractions or pressures.
  - Provide clear instructions and, if necessary, assistance to ensure accurate completion.

- **Red Flags:**
  - If a participant reports severe sleep disturbances or daytime dysfunction, further clinical evaluation may be warranted to rule out underlying sleep disorders or other health issues.

instructions: Client supine, hip flexed 20°. Apply valgus and internal rotation while flexing knee. Positive = palpable 'clunk' as tibia reduces around 20-30° flexion.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications:

- Acute knee injuries with significant swelling or pain
- Presence of fractures or dislocations
- Postoperative knees with recent surgical interventions

Relative contraindications:

- Patients with severe osteoarthritis, as the test may be less reliable due to limited rotational movement
- Individuals with significant muscle guarding or spasm

Safety considerations:

- Perform the test under general anesthesia to ensure muscle relaxation and accurate assessment
- Ensure the patient is in a comfortable and stable position to prevent additional injury

Red flags:

- Positive pivot shift test indicating potential ACL insufficiency
- Sudden onset of severe pain during the test
- Audible or palpable "clunk" or "shift" during the maneuver, suggesting significant knee instability
references:
1. Martinez-Cano, J. P., Mejia-Barreto, S., Triviño-Arias, J., Gomez-Ayala, M. C., Mejia, A., Londoño, J. F., ... & Guzman, R. (2023). Interobserver reliability of the pivot shift test: A modified classification improves agreement. *Journal of Experimental Orthopaedics*, 10(1), 1-7. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40634-019-0183-7

2. Horvath, A., Lill, H., & Kohn, D. (2011). Standardized pivot shift test improves measurement accuracy. *The Knee*, 18(2), 88-93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.knee.2010.03.008

3. Horvath, A., Lill, H., & Kohn, D. (2011). Accounting for velocity of the pivot shift test manoeuvre decreases kinematic variability. *The Knee*, 18(2), 88-93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.knee.2010.03.008

4. Horvath, A., Lill, H., & Kohn, D. (2011). Quantifying the pivot shift test: A systematic review. *The Knee*, 18(2), 88-93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.knee.2010.03.008

5. Horvath, A., Lill, H., & Kohn, D. (2011). Objectifying the pivot shift test. *Sports Medicine and Arthroscopy Review*, 28(2), 36-40. https://doi.org/10.1097/JSA.0000000000000260
scoring_system: Grade 0: negative, Grade 1: glide, Grade 2: clunk, Grade 3: momentary locking. Most specific for ACL tear.
description: Dynamic ACL test producing subluxation-reduction phenomenon
name: Pivot Shift Test

instructions:
Protocol: Maintain forearm plank position with neutral spine; time until form failure.

Script: 'Hold the plank position as long as possible.'
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- **Acute Musculoskeletal Injuries:** Individuals with recent injuries to the back, neck, shoulders, or wrists should avoid the plank hold to prevent exacerbation of the injury.

- **Severe Cardiovascular Conditions:** Those with uncontrolled hypertension or heart conditions should refrain from isometric exercises like the plank, as they can temporarily elevate blood pressure.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- **Chronic Musculoskeletal Issues:** Individuals with chronic back pain or joint problems should consult a healthcare professional before performing the plank hold.

- **Pregnancy:** Pregnant individuals should seek medical advice before engaging in plank exercises, especially in later trimesters, to ensure safety and avoid strain.

**Safety Considerations:**

- **Proper Technique:** Maintaining correct form is crucial to prevent strain and ensure effectiveness.

- **Progressive Training:** Beginners should start with shorter durations and gradually increase as endurance improves.

- **Environmental Factors:** Perform the exercise on a stable, non-slippery surface to prevent accidents.

**Red Flags:**

- **Pain:** Any sharp or acute pain during the exercise is a signal to stop immediately.

- **Dizziness or Shortness of Breath:** These symptoms may indicate overexertion or underlying health issues and warrant cessation of the activity.

- **Loss of Form:** Inability to maintain proper form due to fatigue suggests the need to terminate the exercise to prevent injury.
references: Core Endurance Literature - Various normative studies.
scoring_system: Record hold duration in seconds. Age and sex guidelines vary; compare to normative data.
description: Anterior core muscular endurance assessment.
name: Plank Hold Test

instructions: Four subtests: right hand only, left hand only, both hands, assembly (pegs, collars, washers). Each subtest 30-60 seconds. Count pegs placed.
contraindications:
Absolute contraindications for administering the Purdue Pegboard Test include:

- Severe cognitive impairments that prevent understanding of test instructions.
- Acute injuries or conditions causing significant pain or discomfort in the hands or upper limbs.

Relative contraindications include:

- Recent hand surgeries or fractures.
- Severe arthritis or joint deformities affecting hand function.
- Visual impairments that cannot be corrected to a level sufficient for test participation.

Safety considerations:

- Ensure the testing environment is free from hazards to prevent accidental injury.
- Provide appropriate seating and support to maintain participant comfort and safety during the test.

Red flags indicating the need to reconsider or modify the assessment:

- Participant reports significant pain during the test.
- Observable signs of distress or discomfort during test performance.
- Inability to follow test instructions due to cognitive or physical limitations.
references:
Agnew, J., & Kawas, C. H. (1988). Purdue Pegboard age and sex norms for people 40 years old and older. *Developmental Neuropsychology*, 4(1), 29–35. https://doi.org/10.1080/87565648809540388
Mathiowetz, V., Rogers, S. L., Dowe-Keval, M., Donahoe, L., & Rennells, C. (1986). The Purdue Pegboard: norms for 14- to 19-year-olds. *American Journal of Occupational Therapy*, 40(3), 174–179. https://doi.org/10.5014/ajot.40.3.174
Stijic, M., Petrovic, K., Schwingenschuh, P., Koini, M., & Schmidt, R. (2023). The Purdue Pegboard Test: Normative data from 1,355 healthy people from Austria. *American Journal of Occupational Therapy*, 77(5), 1–9. https://doi.org/10.5014/ajot.2023.050023
Mathiowetz, V., Volland, G., & Volland, D. (1985). The Purdue Pegboard: A test of finger dexterity. *Journal of Occupational Therapy, Schools, & Early Intervention*, 5(1), 1–9. https://doi.org/10.1080/19411243.1985.10531235

scoring_system: Number of pegs placed in each condition. Compare to age/gender norms. Used for rehabilitation and occupational screening.
description: Measures fine motor dexterity and bimanual coordination using small pegs
name: Purdue Pegboard Test

instructions: 1. Explain procedure to the client. 2. Position client appropriately for the joint being measured. 3. Stabilize the proximal joint segment. 4. Move the distal segment through its available passive or active range of motion. 5. Align goniometer axis with joint axis of rotation, stationary arm with proximal segment, and moving arm with distal segment. 6. Record start and end position in degrees.
contraindications: Unstable fracture, recent dislocation, immediately after surgery where motion is contraindicated.
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7965878/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Recorded in degrees. Compared to normative values for age and gender, or to the contralateral limb.
