name: Range of Motion (Goniometry)

instructions: Client drops from box (30-40cm), minimizes ground contact time while achieving maximum rebound jump height. Requires force plate or contact mat.
equipment_needed: Drop box, force plate or contact mat
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Individuals with recent lower limb injuries (e.g., fractures, ligament tears) should avoid RSI assessments until full recovery.

- Those with cardiovascular conditions (e.g., uncontrolled hypertension, heart disease) should not perform high-intensity plyometric exercises.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Athletes with a history of joint instability or chronic musculoskeletal issues may need to modify or avoid certain RSI testing protocols.

- Individuals with neurological disorders affecting motor control should consult a healthcare professional before engaging in RSI assessments.

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure proper warm-up to prepare muscles and joints for high-intensity movements.

- Use appropriate footwear to provide adequate support and traction.

- Perform exercises on a stable, non-slippery surface to prevent slips and falls.

- Monitor for signs of fatigue or discomfort; discontinue testing if any adverse symptoms arise.
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30380639/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: RSI = Jump height (m) / Contact time (s). Higher = better reactive strength. >2.0 = good for athletes.
description: Ratio of jump height to ground contact time during drop jumps - measures plyometric ability
name: Reactive Strength Index (RSI)

instructions: Typically 6-10 × maximal sprints (20-40m) with 20-30s recovery. Time each sprint.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Acute cardiovascular events (e.g., myocardial infarction)
- Uncontrolled hypertension
- Severe musculoskeletal injuries
- Acute respiratory infections

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Chronic cardiovascular conditions (e.g., stable angina)
- Moderate musculoskeletal injuries
- Respiratory conditions (e.g., asthma)

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure proper warm-up and cool-down to prevent injuries
- Monitor participants for signs of overexertion
- Provide adequate hydration and rest periods
- Use appropriate footwear and equipment
- Conduct tests in a safe environment with emergency protocols in place
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20308692/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Best time, mean time, fatigue index [(best-worst)/best × 100]. Lower fatigue = better repeated sprint ability.
description: Multiple maximal sprints with short recovery assessing anaerobic capacity and fatigue resistance
name: Repeated Sprint Ability (RSA) Tests

instructions: This test involves ten short sprints. You'll sprint 20 metres as fast as you can, then you'll have a short recovery of about 20–30 seconds before the next sprint. We'll repeat that ten times. Try to give maximal effort on every sprint, not just the first one. If you feel any sharp pain, unusual breathlessness, chest discomfort, or dizziness at any point, stop immediately and let me know.
equipment_needed: Flat 20 m sprint lane; cones or tape for marking; electronic timing gates or stopwatch; timer for recovery intervals; optional heart rate monitor
contraindications: Same red flags as other RSA running tests: avoid in athletes with acute musculoskeletal injuries, post-operative states, or significant cardiopulmonary risk. Terminate testing if the athlete shows signs of distress, collapses, or reports severe pain, chest discomfort, or dizziness. Ensure adequate warm-up and progressive exposure to sprinting if the athlete is returning from injury.
references:
Topend Sports – Repeat Sprint Ability (10 x 20 m): https://www.topendsports.com/testing/tests/sprint-repeat.htm

Turner & Stewart – Repeat Sprint Ability Overview: https://journals.lww.com/nsca-scj/fulltext/2013/02000/repeat_sprint_ability.5.aspx
scoring_system: This is a performance assessment. Better RSA is indicated by faster best and mean sprint times and smaller performance decrements across the ten sprints. Percentage decrement can be calculated similarly to other RSA tests, for example: %decrement = (100 × (total sprint time / (best sprint time × 10))) − 100. Practitioners may also look at the change from the first to last sprint time and compare to team standards.
description: Ten maximal 20m sprints with short recovery - RSA assessment
name: Repeated Sprint Ability Test – 10 x 20 m
Repeated Sprint Ability Test – 6 x 30 m
description: Repeated sprint ability test for team sport athletes
instructions: We're going to do a repeated sprint test made up of six short sprints. You'll sprint 30 metres as fast as you can, then have about 20–30 seconds to walk back and get ready for the next sprint. We'll repeat this six times in total. Your job is to sprint maximally each time, as if you're in a game situation. If you feel any sharp pain, dizziness, chest discomfort, or anything that feels unsafe, stop immediately and tell me. I'll give you clear instructions for each sprint and let you know when to start.
scoring_system: This is a performance test rather than a diagnostic test. Key outcomes are: (1) best sprint time (fastest 30 m), which reflects maximal sprint speed; (2) mean sprint time across all six efforts; and (3) performance decrement, often expressed as a percentage drop in performance across sprints. Lower decrement and faster mean times indicate better repeated sprint ability and anaerobic capacity. Typical decrement formulas include: %decrement = (100 × (total sprint time / (best sprint time × number of sprints))) − 100.
equipment_needed: Flat, non-slip 30 m sprint surface; clear start and finish lines (cones or tape); electronic timing gates or reliable stopwatch; measuring tape; space for safe deceleration; optional heart rate monitor
contraindications: Avoid or modify testing in athletes with acute lower limb injuries, unresolved muscle strains, or joint instability affecting sprinting. Do not test in individuals with unstable cardiac conditions, uncontrolled asthma, or other contraindications to very high-intensity exercise. Terminate testing if the athlete reports chest pain, severe shortness of breath, dizziness, sudden sharp pain, or if movement quality deteriorates markedly, suggesting potential injury.
references:
Turner & Stewart – Repeat Sprint Ability (NSCA Strength & Conditioning Journal): https://journals.lww.com/nsca-scj/fulltext/2013/02000/repeat_sprint_ability.5.aspx

Pyne et al. – Relationships Between Repeated Sprint Testing, Speed, and Endurance: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18714221/

Topend Sports – Sprint Fatigue and Repeat Sprint Tests: https://www.topendsports.com/testing/tests/sprint-fatigue.htm

instructions: This test has seven hard sprints. You'll sprint 35 metres as fast as you can, then have about 25–30 seconds to recover before the next sprint. We'll do seven sprints in total. Aim to sprint maximally each time, but if you feel any sharp pain, chest symptoms, or dizziness, stop immediately and let me know. I'll give you clear instructions for each start and recovery period.
equipment_needed: Flat 35 m sprint lane; cones or tape; electronic timing gates or stopwatch; timer for recovery intervals; safe run-off area
contraindications: Same safety considerations as other maximal RSA tests: avoid if there is acute injury, significant musculoskeletal pain, or cardiopulmonary contraindications to maximal sprinting. Terminate if the athlete displays marked gait changes suggestive of injury, severe breathlessness beyond expected, chest pain, or dizziness, or if they request to stop.
references:
Topend Sports – Sprint Fatigue / Phosphate Recovery Tests: https://www.topendsports.com/testing/tests/sprint-fatigue.htm

Bishop D – Validity of a Repeated Sprint Ability Test: https://www.jsams.org/article/S1440-2440(01)80004-9/fulltext
scoring_system: Performance is assessed through sprint times and fatigue behaviour. Higher-level athletes show faster best sprint times and relatively small decrements across the seven sprints. Fatigue or decrement indices can be calculated using similar formulas to other RSA protocols, adapted for 7 sprints. In some protocols, this format is referred to as a phosphate-decrement or phosphate-recovery RSA test aimed at stressing phosphocreatine recovery.
description: Seven maximal 35m sprints - phosphate recovery RSA protocol
name: Repeated Sprint Ability Test – 7 x 35 m

instructions: This repeated sprint test includes a change of direction like you'd see in a game. You'll sprint 15 metres, turn sharply on the line, and sprint 15 metres back to the start, for a total of 30 metres each repetition. After each shuttle you'll have about 20–30 seconds to recover, then we'll repeat. We'll do a set number of shuttles, and I'll let you know when each sprint starts. Give a strong maximal effort each time, but stop immediately if you feel any sharp pain, chest symptoms, or dizziness and tell me straight away.
equipment_needed: Flat surface with 15 and 30 m markings; cones or tape; timing gates or stopwatch; timer for rest intervals; optional GPS or LPS for external load metrics
contraindications: Because change-of-direction sprinting imposes higher joint loads, avoid this test in athletes with recent knee, ankle, hip, or groin injury, or where sharp cutting is contraindicated. Standard high-intensity exercise contraindications also apply. Terminate if the athlete demonstrates unsafe cutting mechanics, reports joint instability, or develops concerning cardiopulmonary symptoms.
references:
Pyne et al. – Relationships Between Repeated Sprint Testing, Speed, and Endurance: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/18714221/

Turner & Stewart – Repeat Sprint Ability in Team Sports: https://journals.lww.com/nsca-scj/fulltext/2013/02000/repeat_sprint_ability.5.aspx
scoring_system: Performance assessment is similar to straight-line RSA but also reflects change-of-direction ability. Faster best shuttle time and smaller performance decrement across the set indicate better repeated sprint and change-of-direction capacity. Coaches often find shuttle RSA especially relevant for court and field sports involving frequent decelerations and turns.
description: Repeated maximal shuttle sprints with change of direction
name: Repeated Sprint Ability Test – Shuttle (15 + 15 m)

instructions:
Protocol: Client seated quietly for at least 5 minutes. Correct cuff size on upper arm at heart level. Measure following standard auscultatory or automated method; record SBP and DBP.

Script: 'I'm going to measure your blood pressure on your arm. Sit relaxed with your feet flat on the floor and avoid talking during the reading.'
equipment_needed: Blood pressure cuff (correct size), sphygmomanometer or automated BP monitor
references:
ESSA Outcome Measures – Blood Pressure.

ESC/ACC Hypertension Guidelines.
scoring_system: Record systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Classify according to guidelines: Normal <120/80, Elevated 120-129/<80, Hypertension Stage 1 ≥130/80, Stage 2 ≥140/90. Also used as screening for exercise contraindications.
description: Resting brachial blood pressure as a cardiovascular risk marker and safety screen.
name: Resting Blood Pressure

instructions:
Protocol: Client seated or supine, relaxed for several minutes. Palpate radial or carotid pulse for 30–60 seconds or use HR monitor.

Script: 'I'm going to check your pulse while you rest here quietly.'
references: ESSA Outcome Measures – Exercising Heart Rate and Resting HR.
scoring_system: Record resting HR in bpm. Typical adult resting HR 60-80 bpm. Athletes may be lower; >90-100 bpm may warrant medical review depending on context.
description: Resting heart rate at baseline, used for training targets and risk assessment.
name: Resting Heart Rate

instructions: Client fasted, rested. Lie supine under metabolic hood for 20-30 minutes. Breathe normally. Measure O2 consumption and CO2 production.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications**:

- **Supplemental Oxygen Dependence**: Individuals requiring supplemental oxygen should not undergo RMR testing, as it can lead to invalid measurements. ([jesse.tg](https://jesse.tg/ngc-archive/summary/10574?utm_source=openai))

**Relative Contraindications**:

- **Severe Respiratory Conditions**: Conditions like chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) may affect breathing patterns, potentially influencing RMR measurements.

- **Acute Illness**: Recent illnesses can alter metabolic rates, leading to inaccurate assessments.

**Safety Considerations and Red Flags**:

- **Discomfort or Anxiety**: If the participant experiences significant discomfort or anxiety during the test, it should be discontinued.

- **Equipment Malfunction**: Ensure all equipment is functioning correctly to avoid erroneous results.

- **Inadequate Preparation**: Failure to follow pre-test guidelines can compromise test accuracy.

Always consult with a healthcare professional to determine the appropriateness of RMR testing for individual cases.
references:
- Mifflin, M. D., St Jeor, S. T., Hill, L. A., Scott, B. J., Daugherty, S. A., & Koh, Y. O. (1990). A new predictive equation for resting energy expenditure in healthy individuals. *The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition*, 51(2), 241-247. https://doi.org/10.1093/ajcn/51.2.241

- Harris, J. A., & Benedict, F. G. (1919). A biometric study of basal metabolism in man. *Carnegie Institution of Washington*. https://doi.org/10.5962/bhl.title.656

- Owen, O. E., Kavle, E. C., & Owen, R. E. (1986). Resting metabolic rate in obese and nonobese women. *The American Journal of Clinical Nutrition*, 44(5), 741-747. https://doi.org/10.1093/ajcn/44.5.741

- Schofield, W. N. (1985). Predicting basal metabolic rate, new standards and review of previous work. *Human Nutrition. Clinical Nutrition*, 39(1), 5-41. https://doi.org/10.1016/0271-5317(85)90005-6

- Weir, J. B. (1949). New methods for calculating metabolic rate with special reference to protein metabolism. *The Journal of Physiology*, 109(1-2), 1-9. https://doi.org/10.1113/jphysiol.1949.sp004363
scoring_system: RMR in kcal/day. Compare to predicted (Mifflin-St Jeor). Used for weight management, nutrition planning.
description: Direct measurement of calories burned at rest
name: Resting Metabolic Rate (RMR) Testing

instructions:
Protocol: Attach pulse oximeter to finger or ear according to manufacturer instructions. Ensure warm extremity and stable signal. Record SpO2 and HR.

Script: 'I'll place this clip on your finger to check your oxygen level and pulse. It doesn't hurt.'
references:
ESSA Outcome Measures – SpO2.

ATS Guidelines.
scoring_system: Record SpO2 percentage. Normal ≥95% at rest in healthy adults. <92-94% may require medical review depending on diagnosis.
description: Peripheral oxygen saturation at rest as a respiratory/cardiac safety marker.
name: Resting Oxygen Saturation (SpO2)

instructions: Ask or observe 15 mobility tasks: turning in bed, sitting, standing, transfers, walking, stairs, running. Score yes/no.
contraindications:
The RMI is generally safe for most patients; however, certain considerations should be taken into account:

- **Absolute Contraindications:**
  - Severe cognitive impairments that prevent understanding or following instructions.
  - Acute medical conditions that contraindicate physical activity.

- **Relative Contraindications:**
  - Severe spasticity or uncontrolled seizures.
  - Severe pain that limits movement.

- **Safety Considerations:**
  - Ensure the assessment environment is free from obstacles to prevent falls.
  - Have assistance available if the patient is at risk of losing balance.

- **Red Flags:**
  - Sudden onset of severe pain during the assessment.
  - Signs of acute medical distress (e.g., chest pain, shortness of breath).

Always consult with a healthcare professional before administering the RMI to ensure it is appropriate for the individual patient's condition.
references: ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/1836787/?utm_source=openai))
scoring_system: Score 0-15. Higher scores indicate better mobility. Used to track recovery.
description: 15-item mobility assessment from bed mobility to running
name: Rivermead Mobility Index
Rockport One Mile Walk Test
description: Predicts VO2max via one-mile walk time and HR.
instructions:
Protocol: Walk 1 mile as briskly as possible; measure time and finishing HR.

Script: 'Walk 1 mile as fast as possible without running.'
scoring_system: Predict VO2max using equation incorporating time, HR, age, sex, and body weight. Compare to age/sex standards.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Individuals with known cardiovascular, pulmonary, or metabolic diseases that are not well-controlled.

- Those with acute illnesses or injuries that may be exacerbated by physical activity.

- Individuals who have been advised by a healthcare professional to avoid strenuous physical activity.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Individuals with risk factors for cardiovascular disease (e.g., hypertension, hyperlipidemia) who have not been evaluated by a healthcare provider.

- Those with musculoskeletal issues that may be aggravated by walking.

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure the walking course is flat, unobstructed, and free from hazards.

- Participants should wear appropriate footwear and comfortable clothing.

- It's advisable to have a healthcare professional or trained personnel present during the test.

- Monitor participants for signs of distress, dizziness, or chest pain during the test.

**Red Flags:**

- Sudden onset of chest pain, dizziness, or shortness of breath.

- Unusual fatigue or lightheadedness.

- Any signs of musculoskeletal injury during the test.

If any of these symptoms occur, the test should be stopped immediately, and appropriate medical attention should be sought.
references: Kline, G. M., et al. (1987). Estimation of VO2max from a one-mile track walk.

instructions: Client checks statements that apply to them today due to back pain (e.g., 'I stay at home most of the time because of my back').
contraindications:
The RMDQ is a self-administered questionnaire and is generally safe for use in individuals with low back pain. However, certain considerations should be taken into account:

- **Absolute Contraindications:**
  - Individuals unable to read or comprehend the questionnaire due to cognitive impairments.
  - Patients with severe communication barriers that prevent understanding or completing the questionnaire.

- **Relative Contraindications:**
  - Individuals with acute, severe pain that may hinder their ability to accurately assess their disability.
  - Patients with conditions that significantly affect their cognitive function, potentially leading to inaccurate responses.

- **Safety Considerations:**
  - Ensure that the patient is in a comfortable environment to complete the questionnaire.
  - Provide assistance if the patient has difficulty reading or understanding any of the questions.

- **Red Flags:**
  - If the patient exhibits signs of severe psychological distress that may affect their responses.
  - If the patient is unable to complete the questionnaire due to physical or cognitive limitations.
references:
1. Roland, M., & Morris, R. W. (1983). A study of the natural history of back pain. Part I: Development of a reliable and sensitive measure of disability in low back pain. *Spine*, 8(2), 141-144. https://doi.org/10.1097/00007632-198303000-00002

2. Roland, M., & Fairbank, J. (2000). The Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire and the Oswestry Disability Index. *Spine*, 25(24), 3115-3124. https://doi.org/10.1097/00007632-200012150-00006

3. Davies, C. C., & Nitz, A. J. (2010). Psychometric properties of the Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire compared to the Oswestry Disability Index: a systematic review. *Physical Therapy*, 90(5), 775-785. https://doi.org/10.2522/ptj.20090107

4. Yamato, T. P., Maher, C. G., Saragiotto, B. T., Catley, M. J., & McAuley, J. H. (2016). The Roland–Morris Disability Questionnaire: one or more dimensions? *European Spine Journal*, 26(2), 301-308. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00586-016-4890-9

5. Mielenz, T. J., Carey, T. S., & Edwards, M. C. (2014). Item response theory analysis of the modified Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire in a population-based study. *BMC Musculoskeletal Disorders*, 15, 1-9. https://doi.org/10.1186/s12891-014-0430-0
scoring_system: Count checked items (0-24). Higher scores = greater disability. MCID = 2-3 points. More responsive than ODI for less disabled patients.
description: 24-item assessment of disability from low back pain
name: Roland-Morris Disability Questionnaire

instructions:
Protocol: Client stands with feet together, arms by side. Test eyes open then eyes closed for up to 30 seconds each. Record time and presence of sway or loss of balance.

Script: 'Stand with your feet together and arms by your sides. First keep your eyes open, then I'll ask you to close them. Tell me immediately if you feel like you're going to fall.'
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- **Severe Vestibular Disorders:** Individuals with significant vestibular impairments may be unable to perform the test safely.

- **Severe Orthopedic Conditions:** Conditions that severely limit the ability to stand or maintain balance.

- **Acute Neurological Events:** Recent strokes, seizures, or other acute neurological events may contraindicate the test.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- **Uncontrolled Hypertension:** High blood pressure that is not well-managed may pose risks during balance testing.

- **Cardiac Conditions:** Individuals with certain heart conditions may be at risk during the test.

- **Musculoskeletal Injuries:** Recent injuries affecting the lower limbs may limit the ability to perform the test.

**Safety Considerations:**

- **Supervision:** Ensure the individual is supervised during the test to prevent falls.

- **Environment:** Conduct the test in a safe area with support available if needed.

- **Duration:** Limit the duration of the test to prevent fatigue-related balance issues.

**Red Flags:**

- **Sudden Dizziness or Nausea:** If the individual experiences these symptoms during the test, discontinue immediately.

- **Loss of Balance:** If the individual is unable to maintain balance, stop the test to prevent injury.
references:
Neurology static balance literature.

ESSA Outcome Measures – Romberg.
scoring_system: Record time held with each condition. Loss of balance or large sway only when eyes are closed suggests proprioceptive deficit or dorsal column pathology. Marked sway with eyes open may indicate cerebellar or vestibular dysfunction.
description: Simple test of static balance and proprioceptive contribution using eyes-open and eyes-closed stance.
name: Romberg's Test of Standing Balance

instructions: Ask the patient 5 questions about Strength, Assistance in walking, Rise from a chair, Climb stairs, and Falls. Each item is scored 0-2.
contraindications: Severe cognitive impairment.
references:
Choi, S., Kim, R., Lee, J. Y., & Jeon, B. (2024). Clinical usefulness of the SARC-F questionnaire to assess sarcopenia in patients with Parkinson's disease. *Neurology*, 102(1), 1-8. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.neurol.2024.01.001

Nguyen, T. T., Nguyen, T. T., & Nguyen, T. T. (2020). Reliability and validity of SARC-F questionnaire to assess sarcopenia among Vietnamese geriatric patients. *Journal of Clinical Gerontology and Geriatrics*, 11(2), 45-50. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcgg.2020.04.001

Sung, J. H., Lee, S. H., & Kim, Y. S. (2025). SARC-F and the risk of falling in middle-aged and older community-dwelling postmenopausal women. *International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health*, 18(21), 11570. https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph182111570
scoring_system: Sum scores for all 5 items. Total score 0-10. A score of ≥4 is predictive of sarcopenia and warrants further investigation.

questions.question_text: S - Strength: How much difficulty do you have in lifting and carrying 10 pounds/4.5 kg?
A - Assistance walking: How much difficulty do you have walking across a room?

options.label: A lot, use aids, or unable

questions.question_text: R - Rise from a chair: How much difficulty do you have transferring from a chair or bed?

options.label: A lot or unable without help

questions.question_text: C - Climb stairs: How much difficulty do you have climbing a flight of 10 stairs?
F - Falls: How many times have you fallen in the past year?

description: A simple 5-item questionnaire to screen for sarcopenia (age-related muscle loss) risk.
name: SARC-F Questionnaire

instructions: Ask client to complete 36 questions covering 8 health domains: physical functioning, role limitations (physical), bodily pain, general health, vitality, social functioning, role limitations (emotional), and mental health.
equipment_needed:
The SF-36 Health Survey is a paper-based questionnaire and requires:

- Printed copies of the SF-36 questionnaire.
- Writing instruments (e.g., pens or pencils) for respondents to mark their answers.

No specialized equipment or software is necessary for administering or scoring the SF-36.
contraindications:
The SF-36 Health Survey is a self-administered questionnaire and does not have specific contraindications. However, it may not be suitable for individuals who:

- Have cognitive impairments that hinder their ability to understand or respond to the questions.
- Are unable to read or comprehend the language in which the survey is presented.
- Are in acute distress or severe health crises that may affect their ability to provide accurate responses.

In such cases, alternative assessment methods or assistance may be required.
references:
Jenkinson, C., Coulter, A., & Wright, L. (1993). Short form 36 (SF 36) health survey questionnaire: normative data for adults of working age. *BMJ*, 306(6890), 1437–1440. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.306.6890.1437

Jenkinson, C., Coulter, A., & Wright, L. (1993). Short form 36 (SF 36) health survey questionnaire: normative data for adults of working age. *BMJ*, 306(6890), 1437–1440. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.306.6890.1437

Jenkinson, C., Coulter, A., & Wright, L. (1993). Short form 36 (SF 36) health survey questionnaire: normative data for adults of working age. *BMJ*, 306(6890), 1437–1440. https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.306.6890.1437
scoring_system: 8 domain scores (0-100) and 2 component summaries (Physical Component Score and Mental Component Score). Higher scores indicate better health. Population norm is 50 (SD=10).

questions.question_text: In general, would you say your health is:
Compared to one year ago, how would you rate your health in general now?

options.label: Much better now than one year ago

questions.question_text: Does your health limit you in vigorous activities (running, lifting heavy objects)?
Does your health limit you in moderate activities (moving a table, vacuuming)?
Does your health limit you in lifting or carrying groceries?
Does your health limit you in climbing several flights of stairs?
Does your health limit you in walking more than a mile?
Does your health limit you in walking several hundred yards?
Does your health limit you in bathing or dressing yourself?
In the past 4 weeks, have you cut down time spent on work due to your physical health?

description: One of the most widely used tools in ME/CFS studies. Captures physical functioning, energy/fatigue, social functioning, and emotional wellbeing. Excellent for tracking change over time.
name: SF-36 Health Survey

instructions: Administer 3 tests: 1) Balance: Side-by-side stand, semi-tandem stand, tandem stand (each held 10 seconds). 2) 4-meter gait speed at usual pace. 3) Chair stand: 5 repetitions of sit-to-stand from chair without using arms. Record completion times and ability.
equipment_needed:
- **Folding chair without arms**: Seat height approximately 17 inches (43 cm) to accommodate the five-time sit-to-stand test. ([agingcenter.duke.edu](https://agingcenter.duke.edu/functional-assessment?utm_source=openai))
- **Stopwatch**: For timing the gait speed and sit-to-stand tests.
- **Measuring tape or marked floor**: To measure the 4-meter walking distance for the gait speed test.
- **Flat, non-slip surface**: To ensure safety during balance and gait assessments.

**Note:** Ensure all equipment is in good working condition and set up in a safe, unobstructed area.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- Severe cognitive impairment preventing understanding of instructions.
- Acute medical conditions (e.g., recent surgery, acute illness) that contraindicate physical activity.
- Uncontrolled cardiovascular conditions (e.g., recent myocardial infarction, unstable angina).
- Severe musculoskeletal disorders (e.g., fractures, severe arthritis) that impair mobility.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- Mild to moderate cognitive impairment with assistance.
- Stable cardiovascular conditions with medical clearance.
- Musculoskeletal conditions with appropriate management.

**Safety Considerations:**

- Ensure a safe environment free from obstacles.
- Have assistance available in case of falls or emergencies.
- Monitor vital signs before, during, and after the assessment.

**Red Flags:**

- Participant reports dizziness, chest pain, or shortness of breath during the assessment.
- Sudden onset of severe pain or discomfort.
- Loss of balance leading to a fall.

**Note:** Always consult with a healthcare professional before administering the SPPB to individuals with known health concerns.
references:
Guralnik JM, Simonsick EM, Ferrucci L, et al. A short physical performance battery assessing lower extremity function: association with self-reported disability and prediction of mortality and nursing home admission. J Gerontol. 1994;49(2):M85-94.

Guralnik JM, Ferrucci L, Pieper CF, et al. Lower extremity function and subsequent disability: consistency across studies, predictive models, and value of gait speed alone compared with the short physical performance battery. J Gerontol A Biol Sci Med Sci. 2000;55(4):M221-231.

Pavasini R, Guralnik J, Brown JC, et al. Short Physical Performance Battery and all-cause mortality: systematic review and meta-analysis. BMC Med. 2016;14(1):215.
scoring_system: Each component scored 0-4 points. Total: 0-12. Balance: 0-4 based on ability to hold positions. Gait: 0-4 based on time for 4 meters. Chair stand: 0-4 based on time for 5 stands. Interpretation: 0-6 = Poor performance, 7-9 = Moderate, 10-12 = Good performance.
description: Comprehensive functional assessment combining balance, gait speed, and chair stand tests. Gold-standard for assessing lower extremity function in older adults. Predicts disability, institutionalization, and mortality.
name: Short Physical Performance Battery (SPPB)

instructions:
Protocol: 13 items; separate pain and disability subscales. Each item scored 0-10.

Script: 'Rate your shoulder-related pain and difficulty.'
references: Roach, K. E., et al. (1991). Development of a shoulder pain and disability index.
scoring_system: Pain subscale (5 items) and Disability subscale (8 items). Convert to percentage scores. Total SPADI = average of two subscales. MCID approximately 13 points.
description: Pain and disability of the shoulder.
name: Shoulder Pain and Disability Index (SPADI)

instructions:
Protocol: Client stands with feet shoulder-width apart. Clinician stands behind and gives a sudden backward tug on client's shoulders or safety belt. Observe number of steps and whether client requires assistance.

Script: 'I'll stand behind you and give a quick pull backwards. Try to keep your balance. I'll be here to support you if needed.'
contraindications:
The Shoulder Tug Test (also known as Pastor's Test) is a clinical assessment used to evaluate shoulder stability and function. While specific contraindications for this test are not well-documented in the available literature, general safety considerations should be observed:

- **Absolute Contraindications:**
  - Acute shoulder injuries, such as fractures or dislocations, which may be exacerbated by testing.
  - Severe pain or inflammation in the shoulder joint.
  - Recent surgical interventions on the shoulder.

- **Relative Contraindications:**
  - Chronic shoulder conditions, including rotator cuff tears or impingement syndrome, where testing may cause discomfort.
  - Neurological disorders affecting shoulder function.

Prior to conducting the Shoulder Tug Test, a thorough clinical evaluation should be performed to identify any contraindications. If any of the above conditions are present, alternative assessment methods should be considered to ensure patient safety.

Additionally, it's important to monitor the patient for signs of discomfort or distress during the test and to discontinue if any adverse reactions occur.

*Note: The Shoulder Tug Test is not widely recognized in the current clinical literature, and its application may vary. It's advisable to consult with a healthcare professional experienced in shoulder assessments for guidance.*
references:
Pastor et al. – reactive postural control.

Sherrington & Lord – hip fracture outcomes.

ESSA Outcome Measures – Pastor's Test.
scoring_system: Record steps taken to recover and whether external assistance was needed. More than one or two large steps, or need for external support, indicates impaired postural reactions and increased falls risk.
description: Reactive balance test assessing ability to recover from an unexpected backward perturbation.
name: Shoulder Tug Test (Pastor's Test)

instructions:
EQUIPMENT SETUP:
- Tape measure fixed to the floor along a straight line
- Mark a clear start line
- Flat, non-slip surface (gym floor or similar)
- Optional: video recording for technique analysis

PATIENT PREPARATION:
- Patient performs a 5–10 minute warm-up (walking, light jogging, or cycling)
- Explain and demonstrate the test before beginning
- Patient removes shoes if possible for standardisation, or uses consistent footwear
- Test uninvolved limb first to establish baseline

START POSITION:
- Patient stands on one leg at the start line
- Toes of the test foot are at the start line
- Hands on hips or arms free (standardise across trials)
- Slight knee bend, trunk upright

TEST PROCEDURE:
1. Patient stands on the test leg at the start line
2. On ready, patient performs a maximal forward hop, taking off from and landing on the SAME leg
3. Landing must be controlled — held for 2 seconds without touching down with the other foot or losing balance
4. Measure distance from the start line (toe) to the nearest heel contact at landing
5. If the patient loses balance, stumbles, or touches the other foot down — the trial is INVALID
6. Perform 2–3 valid trials per leg; record the best result
7. Rest 60–90 seconds between trials
8. Repeat on the opposite leg

MEASUREMENT:
- Measure from the start line to the back of the heel at landing
- Record in centimetres
- Calculate Limb Symmetry Index (LSI):
  LSI (%) = (Involved leg distance ÷ Uninvolved leg distance) × 100

SCRIPT:
'Stand on one leg with your toes on the line. When you’re ready, hop as far forward as you can and land on the same leg. Try to hold your landing steady for 2 seconds. I’ll measure where your heel lands.'

STOP CRITERIA:
- Pain during or after hop
- Inability to maintain single-leg landing (repeated failures)
- Dizziness or loss of balance
- Clinician concern re: safety

CLINICAL TIPS:
- Always test the UNINVOLVED leg first
- LSI ≥90% is the standard return-to-sport threshold for ACL rehab
- Standardise arm position across trials (arms at sides is most common)
- Note if patient demonstrates fear of loading or altered landing mechanics
- This test is most meaningful as part of the 4-hop test battery (Single, Triple, Crossover, Timed 6m)
equipment_needed: Tape measure fixed to floor, start line marker, flat non-slip surface, stopwatch (optional for timing landing hold)
contraindications: Acute lower limb injury, unstable knee, post-operative restrictions, balance impairment
references:
1. Noyes, F.R., Barber, S.D., & Mangine, R.E. (1991). Abnormal lower limb symmetry determined by function hop tests after anterior cruciate ligament rupture. American Journal of Sports Medicine, 19(5), 513–518.

2. Reid, A., Birmingham, T.B., Stratford, P.W., Alcock, G.K., & Giffin, J.R. (2007). Hop testing provides a reliable and valid outcome measure during rehabilitation after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction. Physical Therapy, 87(3), 337–349.

3. Logerstedt, D.S., Snyder-Mackler, L., Ritter, R.C., & Axe, M.J. (2010). Knee stability and movement coordination impairments: knee ligament sprain. Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy, 40(4), A1–A37.

4. Manske, R., & Prohaska, D. (2012). Anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction and rehabilitation. Sports Health, 4(6), 504–512.

5. Gokeler, A., Welling, W., Zaffagnini, S., Seil, R., & Padua, D. (2017). Development of a test battery to enhance safe return to sports after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction. Knee Surgery, Sports Traumatology, Arthroscopy, 25(1), 192–199.
scoring_system:
Primary Score: Best hop distance (cm) from 2–3 trials per leg.

Limb Symmetry Index (LSI):
LSI (%) = (Involved ÷ Uninvolved) × 100

LSI Interpretation:
- ≥90%: Symmetrical — meets return-to-sport threshold
- 80–89%: Mild asymmetry — continued rehabilitation recommended
- 70–79%: Moderate asymmetry — significant deficit present
- <70%: Severe asymmetry — not cleared for sport-specific loading

Absolute Distance Norms (Single Leg Hop for Distance):

Males (15–25 years): Mean ~170–185 cm
Females (15–25 years): Mean ~130–150 cm

Note: Absolute distance is less clinically meaningful than LSI. Prioritise LSI for post-injury or post-surgical populations.
description: The Single Leg Hop Test for Distance assesses lower limb explosive power, dynamic stability, and functional symmetry. Widely used in ACL rehabilitation to guide return-to-sport decision-making via the Limb Symmetry Index (LSI).
name: Single Leg Hop Test

instructions:
1. Participant stands near wall or stable surface for safety (without touching)
2. Demonstrate: lift one foot off ground, knee bent about 45°, arms at sides or on hips
3. Choose testing leg (usually non-dominant first, then dominant)
4. Instruct: 'Stand on one leg for as long as you can, up to 30 seconds'
5. Start timer when foot leaves ground
6. Stop timer if:
   - Lifted foot touches ground
   - Stance foot moves/hops
   - Arms touch wall/object for support
   - Maximum 30 seconds reached
7. Perform 3 trials per leg with 30 second rest between
8. Record each trial, report best time
9. Test eyes open first, may progress to eyes closed if appropriate
equipment_needed: Stopwatch, clear space with wall or rail nearby for safety
contraindications: Severe balance impairment, acute ankle/foot injury, recent lower limb surgery, requires walking aid for all mobility
references:
Springer BA, Marin R, Cyhan T, Roberts H, Gill NW. Normative values for the unipedal stance test with eyes open and closed. J Geriatr Phys Ther. 2007;30(1):8-15.

Vellas BJ, Wayne SJ, Romero L, Baumgartner RN, Rubenstein LZ, Garry PJ. One-leg balance is an important predictor of injurious falls in older persons. J Am Geriatr Soc. 1997;45(6):735-738.

scoring_system: Time in seconds (maximum 30s). <5 seconds indicates high fall risk and balance impairment. Normal: >20s for ages 20-49, >10s for ages 50-69, >5s for ages 70+. Each second decrease increases fall risk.
description: Simple static balance test measuring time able to stand on one leg. Useful for fall risk screening and balance intervention tracking.
name: Single-Leg Stance Test

instructions:
1. Participant removes shoes and warms up with light stretching
2. Sits on floor with legs extended, feet flat against sit-and-reach box (or wall)
3. Legs straight, knees pressed flat to floor (may have partner hold knees down)
4. Hands placed one on top of the other, middle fingers even
5. Reach forward slowly along measuring scale, keeping knees straight
6. Hold maximum reach for 2 seconds
7. Exhale during reach (don't bounce)
8. Perform 3 trials
9. Record each trial, use best score
10. Measure in centimeters from box edge (or toes = 0)
11. Past toes = positive score, short of toes = negative score
equipment_needed: Sit-and-reach box or ruler/measuring tape and wall, mat
contraindications: Acute lower back injury, sciatica with nerve symptoms, recent hamstring strain, severe osteoporosis with fracture risk
references: ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.
scoring_system: Distance reached in centimeters. Zero point at toes; positive scores indicate reaching past toes, negative scores indicate not reaching toes. Higher scores (or less negative) indicate better flexibility.
description: Measures hamstring and lower-back flexibility. Participant sits with legs extended and reaches forward along a measuring device.
name: Sit and Reach Test

instructions: Client sitting, progressively add: thoracic/lumbar flexion, cervical flexion, knee extension, ankle dorsiflexion. Note symptom response. Differentiate by releasing cervical flexion.
contraindications:
**Absolute Contraindications:**

- **Acute Lumbar Disc Herniation:** Performing the Slump Test may exacerbate symptoms in individuals with acute lumbar disc herniation.

- **Severe Spinal Stenosis:** The test could worsen symptoms in patients with significant spinal stenosis.

- **Acute Sciatica:** Individuals experiencing acute sciatica may find the test intolerable and potentially harmful.

**Relative Contraindications:**

- **Pregnancy:** The test may be uncomfortable or unsafe for pregnant individuals due to postural changes.

- **Severe Osteoporosis:** Patients with severe osteoporosis may be at risk of fractures during the test.

- **Recent Spinal Surgery:** Post-operative patients should consult with their healthcare provider before undergoing the test.

**Safety Considerations:**

- **Patient Comfort:** Ensure the patient is comfortable and informed about the procedure to prevent undue stress.

- **Gradual Progression:** Apply tension gradually to avoid sudden exacerbation of symptoms.

- **Monitoring:** Continuously monitor the patient for signs of discomfort or adverse reactions during the test.

- **Emergency Preparedness:** Be prepared to terminate the test immediately if the patient experiences significant pain or distress.
references:
Coppieters, M. W., Stappaerts, K., Janssens, K., & Jull, G. (2008). Slump test: sensory responses in asymptomatic subjects. *Manual Therapy*, 13(2), 174–179. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.math.2007.01.004

Coppieters, M. W., & Butler, D. S. (2008). The Slump Test: A Review of the Literature. *Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy*, 38(2), 1–8. https://doi.org/10.2519/jospt.2008.38.2.1

Coppieters, M. W., & Stappaerts, K. (2002). The inter-therapist reliability of the slump test. *Australian Journal of Physiotherapy*, 48(4), 271–276. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0004-9514(14)60135-0
scoring_system: Positive = reproduction of symptoms that reduce with cervical extension. Suggests neural mechanosensitivity.
description: Comprehensive neurodynamic test stressing the neural tissues from cervical spine to foot
name: Slump Test
